text: High-performing 10-km runners adopt a reverse-J pacing strategy: a fast start (~18.8 km/h), gradual deceleration to 2 km, steady pace through 9.6 km, then an end-spurt.
Lower-performing 10-km runners adopt a more even pacing strategy, starting at a velocity not significantly different from their average race pace, with a slight end-spurt.
Peak treadmill running velocity, running economy, and lactate threshold are significant determinants of pacing strategy selection in a 10-km race.
Higher-performing runners start a 10-km race significantly above their average race velocity, while lower-performing runners start near their average race velocity.
Both high- and low-performing 10-km runners produce an end-spurt in the final 400 m, suggesting this is a universal pacing feature regardless of performance level.

Net blood lactate accumulation at 15 km/h is significantly correlated with start, middle, last, and average velocities during a 10-km race, indicating anaerobic capacity influences pacing across all race phases.
